approaches.label: Thaller (Ivory Stack)
approaches.description_en: Manfred Thaller's conceptual design for historical information systems, based on co-references, token strings, and multi-source files (MSF). Design-level, not implemented as formal ontology.
approaches.note: Based on design (Ivory Stack 2018–2021), not on implementation.

approaches.label: SDHSS
approaches.description_en: Semantic Data for Humanities and Social Sciences. CIDOC-CRM extension for prosopography and historical research. Uses temporal entities and assertion-based architecture.

approaches.label: CIDOC-CRM
approaches.description_en: Conceptual Reference Model for cultural heritage documentation. Event-centric ontology. ISO 21127. Broad scope, not specifically designed for historical research methodology.

approaches.label: Bookkeeping Ontology
approaches.description_en: Domain-specific ontology for historical bookkeeping records. CIDOC-CRM based. Focused on economic transactions, currencies, and accounting practices.

approaches.label: Factoid Model
approaches.description_en: Prosopographic data model based on factoids — atomic statements about persons extracted from sources. Designed for person-centric historical databases.

approaches.label: STAR
approaches.description_en: Spatiotemporal Archaeological Resource model. Focused on archaeological excavation data, stratigraphic relationships, and material culture.

approaches.label: PROV-O
approaches.description_en: W3C Provenance Ontology. General-purpose provenance model for tracking the origin and derivation of data. Not domain-specific to historical research.

R-E1.1.justification: Co-references with probabilistic/possibilistic weights provide graded uncertainty as a system-level feature. All data is inherently fuzzy.

R-E1.2.justification: Co-reference mechanism allows multiple interpretations with different weights to coexist. Contradictions may resist resolution by design.

R-E1.3.justification: Incompleteness taxonomy (Ex 6.1–6.3) with Explicit Defaults and Triggered Defaults. Most detailed absence treatment of all approaches.

R-E2.1.justification: Multi-source files (MSF) track which source contributed each piece of information. Provenance is a core architectural feature.

R-E2.2.justification: Epistemic distance is not modeled. Co-references track source attribution but not the degree of mediation.

R-E3.1.justification: Multiple co-references from different sources for the same referent coexist without forced resolution.

R-E3.2.justification: No typed relations between perspectives. Co-references coexist but their mutual relationships are not expressed.

R-E4.1.justification: Interpretation context can be attached to co-references but is not a first-class construct with dedicated metadata fields.

R-E5.1.justification: Temporal categorization is not systematically addressed. Token strings preserve original forms but do not track when categories become anachronistic.

R-E5.2.justification: No explicit emic/etic distinction mechanism. Categories are preserved as tokens but their provenance (actor vs. researcher) is not systematically tracked.

R-M1.1.justification: Source types are explicitly distinguished and affect processing. MSF architecture differentiates source characteristics.

R-M2.1.justification: Creation purpose can be recorded as source metadata but is not a dedicated modeling construct.

R-M2.2.justification: Incompleteness taxonomy addresses missing data generally but does not specifically model systematic omissions as a source-type property.

R-M3.1.justification: Incompleteness taxonomy directly addresses transmission gaps. Lacunae are modelable through the default mechanisms.

R-M4.1.justification: Token string architecture inherently separates the physical token from its interpreted content.

R-M4.2.justification: Material properties can be recorded but are not given the same systematic treatment as textual content.

R-S1.1.justification: Temporal modeling distinguishes creation time, event time, and annotation time as separate dimensions.

R-S1.2.justification: Token strings preserve historical reference systems (currencies, measurements) in their original form alongside modern equivalents.

R-S2.1.justification: Token strings can preserve diplomatic readings, but the systematic relationship between diplomatic and normalized forms is not a dedicated construct.

R-S3.1.justification: Source relations can be modeled through references between MSF entries but lack a typed relation vocabulary.

R-A1.justification: Core architectural principle: 'Represents the artifacts as free from any interpretation as possible.' Token strings vs. co-references implement this separation.

R-A2.justification: Not explicitly event-centric. Thaller's architecture organizes around sources and co-references, not events as primary units.

R-A3.justification: 'Accepts that contradictions may resist resolution' — monotonic by design, new information added without modifying existing data.

R-A4.justification: 'All interpretations represent tendencies, no certainties' — assertion-based architecture with source attribution and confidence.

R-E1.1.justification: Certainty can be expressed through E13 qualifiers but uncertainty is not a pervasive system feature with dedicated constructs.

R-E1.2.justification: Multiple E13 assignments possible for competing interpretations but this is not a primary design pattern with dedicated support.

R-E1.3.justification: No dedicated mechanism for representing known unknowns. Open World Assumption treats missing data as unknown, not as informationally significant.

R-E2.1.justification: E13 Attribute Assignment links every assertion to its source through P140 assigned attribute to / P141 assigned.

R-E2.2.justification: No dedicated epistemic distance concept. Derivation chains provide some proxy but no systematic distance measurement.

R-E3.1.justification: Multiple Attribute Assignments from different scholars can coexist for the same entity. Core prosopographic feature.

R-E3.2.justification: No vocabulary for typed relationships between perspectives (agreement, contradiction, subsumption).

R-E4.1.justification: E13 records who and when but not under which theoretical framework or with what interpretive purpose. Incomplete metadata.

R-E5.1.justification: Temporal validity of types can be modeled through E55 Type with temporal qualifiers, but not a primary design focus.

R-E5.2.justification: No emic/etic distinction. E55 Type is used for all classifications without systematic provenance tracking.

R-M1.1.justification: Inherits CIDOC-CRM's rich source type hierarchy through E73 Information Object subclasses.

R-M2.1.justification: Creation purpose can be modeled through E65 Creation events but is not a dedicated field.

R-M2.2.justification: No mechanism for representing systematic omissions. Source limitations are not modeled as source properties.

R-M3.1.justification: Transmission gaps cannot be explicitly represented. Lost sources are simply absent from the model.

R-M4.1.justification: CIDOC-CRM's E18 Physical Thing / E73 Information Object distinction provides carrier-content separation.

R-M4.2.justification: Material properties can be described through E54 Dimension and condition reports but are not integrated with content interpretation.

R-S1.1.justification: CIDOC-CRM temporal entities (E2 Temporal Entity, E52 Time-Span) distinguish event time, creation time, and documentation time.

R-S1.2.justification: Historical reference systems can theoretically be recorded but dual representation is not built in or systematically supported.

R-S2.1.justification: No dedicated mechanism for diplomatic vs. normalized readings. Text variants are not a modeling focus.

R-S3.1.justification: Source relations can be modeled through P130 shows features of, P67 refers to, and other CRM properties.

R-A1.justification: Separation of observation (source) from interpretation (assertion) is an architectural principle inherited from CIDOC-CRM's E13.

R-A2.justification: Event-centric by design (CIDOC-CRM foundation). E5 Event is the primary organizational unit.

R-A3.justification: RDF-based model is inherently additive. New triples can be added without modifying existing ones.

R-A4.justification: E13 Attribute Assignment wraps all interpretive claims in assertions with agent, source, and optional certainty.

R-E1.1.justification: E13 Attribute Assignment can carry certainty annotations, but uncertainty is not a pervasive system feature. Often treated as optional metadata.

R-E1.2.justification: Multiple E13 assignments possible but the pattern is not enforced or promoted as standard practice.

R-E1.3.justification: No mechanism for known unknowns. OWA treats missing information as simply unknown.

R-E2.1.justification: E13 Attribute Assignment provides provenance linkage. P140/P141 connect assertions to sources.

R-E2.2.justification: No dedicated epistemic distance concept. Can be approximated through chains of derivation events.

R-E3.1.justification: Multiple E13 assignments can coexist but the model does not specifically promote multiperspectivity as a design principle.

R-E3.2.justification: No vocabulary for expressing relationships between competing perspectives.

R-E4.1.justification: E13 records performer and time but not theoretical framework or interpretive purpose.

R-E5.1.justification: Temporal contingency of categories is not addressed by the type system.

R-E5.2.justification: No emic/etic distinction in the type system.

R-M1.1.justification: Rich hierarchy of information object classes (E31 Document, E36 Visual Item, etc.) with source type semantics.

R-M2.1.justification: E65 Creation can link to motivation/purpose through P17 was motivated by, but it is generic.

R-M2.2.justification: No concept of systematic omissions. Source biases are not modeled.

R-M3.1.justification: No mechanism for explicitly representing that sources existed but are lost.

R-M4.1.justification: E18 Physical Thing / E73 Information Object distinction is a foundational feature.

R-M4.2.justification: E54 Dimension can describe material properties but integration with content interpretation is not systematic. Material as evidence is possible but not promoted.

R-S1.1.justification: Temporal entities with E52 Time-Span provide multi-layered temporal representation. Core CRM strength.

R-S1.2.justification: E58 Measurement Unit exists but dual representation of historical/modern systems is not built in.

R-S2.1.justification: No mechanism for diplomatic vs. normalized text readings.

R-S3.1.justification: P130 shows features of, P67 refers to, P128 carries provide source relation vocabulary.

R-A1.justification: E13 Attribute Assignment separates observation from interpretation at the architectural level.

R-A2.justification: Fundamentally event-centric. E5 Event is the backbone of the model.

R-A3.justification: RDF base is additive, but CRM itself does not enforce monotonicity — deletions and modifications are possible.

R-A4.justification: E13 exists but is rarely mandatory. Most CRM usage does not wrap all claims in assertions with confidence and source.

R-E1.1.justification: Inherits CRM's E13 pattern but uncertainty is not a primary concern in the bookkeeping domain design.

R-E1.2.justification: Designed for relatively unambiguous economic transactions. Alternative interpretations are not a focus.

R-E1.3.justification: No mechanism for explicit knowledge gaps beyond inherited CRM limitations.

R-E2.1.justification: Provenance to source ledgers is a core feature. Each transaction traces back to its folio/entry.

R-E2.2.justification: Epistemic distance is not relevant for the bookkeeping domain as modeled.

R-E3.1.justification: Single-source transcription model. Multiperspectivity is not a design consideration.

R-E4.1.justification: Interpretation metadata beyond basic provenance is not modeled.

R-E5.1.justification: Historical economic categories (Währungen, Maßeinheiten) are temporally bounded in the domain model.

R-M1.1.justification: Source type can be indicated through CRM inheritance but is not a primary dimension of the bookkeeping-specific design.

R-M2.1.justification: The accounting purpose of each document type is modeled as part of the domain ontology.

R-M2.2.justification: Systematic omissions (e.g., informal transactions not recorded) are not modeled.

R-M3.1.justification: Lost ledgers or missing years are not explicitly representable.

R-M4.1.justification: Inherited CRM carrier-content separation but not a primary focus.

R-M4.2.justification: Material properties of account books are not modeled as informationally significant.

R-S1.1.justification: Transaction dates, recording dates, and accounting periods are explicitly distinguished.

R-S1.2.justification: Historical currencies are referenced in the domain but systematic dual representation with conversion mechanisms is not a core feature.

R-S3.1.justification: Relations between source documents are not a focus.

R-A1.justification: Some separation through CRM inheritance but transcription and interpretation are often conflated in practice.

R-A2.justification: Transactions are central domain objects but the model is not event-centric in the generic CRM/historical sense.

R-A3.justification: Inherited from CRM/RDF but not explicitly enforced.

R-A4.justification: Assertions are not a modeling feature. Data is treated as factual transcription.

R-E1.1.justification: Factoids can carry certainty qualifiers but this is implementation-dependent, not a core model feature.

R-E1.2.justification: Multiple factoids from different sources can represent competing claims about the same person. Core design feature.

R-E1.3.justification: No mechanism for representing known unknowns. Missing data is simply absent.

R-E2.1.justification: Each factoid links to its source. Provenance is the foundational principle of the factoid model.

R-E3.1.justification: Factoids from different sources coexist without resolution. The model is designed for contradictory information.

R-E3.2.justification: No mechanism for expressing relationships between factoids beyond shared referent.

R-E4.1.justification: Factoids track source but not the interpreter's framework or purpose.

R-M1.1.justification: Source type can be recorded as metadata on the source entity but is not a modeling dimension.

R-M2.1.justification: Source creation purpose is not modeled.

R-M3.1.justification: Lost sources are not explicitly representable.

R-S1.1.justification: Dates are recorded on factoids but multiple temporal layers are not systematically distinguished.

R-S1.2.justification: Historical reference systems are not modeled.

R-S3.1.justification: Source relations beyond shared referent are not modeled.

R-A1.justification: Factoids separate source from interpretation implicitly but not as explicitly as CRM's E13.

R-A3.justification: Factoids are conceptually additive but implementations may allow modification. Monotonicity is not enforced at the model level.

R-A4.justification: The factoid itself IS an assertion: person + source + claim. Core architectural principle.

R-E1.1.justification: Certainty is not a systematic feature. Archaeological recording assumes factual documentation.

R-E1.2.justification: Alternative interpretations are not a core modeling feature.

R-E1.3.justification: No explicit mechanism for known unknowns in the archaeological record.

R-E2.1.justification: Every observation links to its excavation context and finds record. Provenance is fundamental to archaeological documentation.

R-E2.2.justification: Epistemic distance is not modeled in the archaeological recording context.

R-E3.1.justification: Designed for single-team documentation, not multi-perspective coexistence.

R-E4.1.justification: Excavation metadata (who, when) exists but interpretive framework tracking is not modeled.

R-E5.1.justification: Period classifications exist but temporal contingency of categories is not addressed.

R-M1.1.justification: Material culture types (ceramic, metal, bone, etc.) are a primary dimension.

R-M2.1.justification: Creation purpose of material culture is not systematically modeled.

R-M2.2.justification: Taphonomic bias is acknowledged but not modeled as systematic omission.

R-M3.1.justification: Destroyed archaeological contexts (e.g., by later construction) are not explicitly representable.

R-M4.1.justification: Physical object and its informational content are separated in the finds documentation model.

R-M4.2.justification: Material analysis is recorded in finds documentation but as descriptive metadata rather than as a systematically integrated informational dimension.

R-S1.1.justification: Stratigraphic time, absolute dating, and recording time are distinguished.

R-S1.2.justification: Archaeological dating systems (BP, BC/AD, typological periods) coexist but dual representation is not systematic.

R-S2.1.justification: Not applicable — STAR deals with material culture, not text readings.

R-S3.1.justification: Stratigraphic relationships have different semantics from historical source relations. Not applicable.

R-A1.justification: Recording separates observation from interpretation in excavation methodology but the model conflates them partially.

R-A2.justification: Archaeological events (depositions, destructions) are central organizational units.

R-A3.justification: Stratigraphic reinterpretation can require modifying existing records.

R-A4.justification: Data is typically recorded as facts, not assertions with confidence levels.

R-E1.1.justification: No uncertainty mechanism. PROV-O tracks derivation, not certainty.

R-E2.1.justification: Provenance is the core purpose. wasDerivedFrom, wasAttributedTo, wasGeneratedBy provide full provenance chains.

R-E2.2.justification: Derivation chains can encode distance implicitly but it is not a dedicated concept.

R-E3.1.justification: Single-provenance model. Not designed for multiperspectivity.

R-E4.1.justification: wasAssociatedWith links to agents but interpretive framework, purpose, and theoretical context are not modeled.

R-M1.1.justification: No source type concept. Entities are generic.

R-M2.1.justification: No creation purpose concept beyond generic Activity.

R-S1.1.justification: Timestamps exist but multi-layered temporal representation is not a feature.

R-S3.1.justification: wasDerivedFrom can link entities but lacks typed historical source relations.

R-A1.justification: No separation of source representation from interpretation.

R-A2.justification: Activity-centric but activities are generic, not event-centric in the historical sense.

R-A3.justification: Provenance records are inherently additive. New derivations do not modify existing ones.

R-A4.justification: wasAttributedTo links entities to agents, providing partial assertion-like attribution, but without confidence levels or source links.

coverage.scoring_method: Coverage = (structural + metadata) / 24 * 100, rounded to nearest integer. Both structural and metadata count as 'addressed' (coverage). Absent = not addressed.

textual_sources.description: Weighted for textual historical sources: epistemic and semiotic requirements weighted higher (C=3 for core epistemic, R=2 for representation/semiotic, M=1 for medial/structural)
textual_sources.note: C=3 for epistemic (core requirements), R=2 for semiotic (representation), M=1 for medial and structural
